k-eCommerce.history: k-eCommerce is an SMB-focused ecommerce platform that integrates with ERP systems including Microsoft Dynamics, SAP Business One, and Acumatica. It was acquired by Mediagrif in 2019 and is now operated under the mdf commerce brand, a publicly traded Canadian digital commerce company.
k-eCommerce.summary_abuse: k-eCommerce is a legitimate B2B ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. It targets SMBs with ERP integrations and has no characteristics that make it a preferred tool for fraudsters.

K-Sup.history: K-Sup was created by Kosmos, a French company founded in 1998 and headquartered in Nantes, France, as an open-source CMS and digital workspace (ENT) specifically designed for higher education and research institutions. Built on Java, it supports natively multisite, multilingual, and multichannel deployments, enabling universities to manage complex web infrastructure from a single platform. Kosmos has deployed K-Sup at institutions including the Université Libre de Bruxelles.
K-Sup.summary_abuse: K-Sup is a niche open-source CMS deployed exclusively at universities and research institutions, managed by IT departments rather than end users. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a self-hosted backend platform with no consumer-facing content hosting surface that would attract phishers or scammers.

K2.history: K2 was founded in 2000 in Johannesburg, South Africa by Adriaan van Wyk and Olaf Wagner. Received $150M from Francisco Partners in 2015. Renamed to K2 Software Inc in 2020 and acquired by Nintex in October 2020. Now Nintex K2 Cloud. Used by enterprises for workflow automation on SharePoint.
K2.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Legitimate enterprise BPM platform acquired by Nintex. ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II certified.

Kadence WP Blocks.history: Kadence Blocks is a WordPress Gutenberg block plugin developed by Ben Ritner, founded 2013. Acquired by Liquid Web/iThemes. It was one of the top WordPress block plugins with over 300,000 active installs. Ben Ritner stepped back from the project in late 2024 amid concerns about the acquisition direction.
Kadence WP Blocks.summary_abuse: No significant abuse patterns. Open-source plugin (GPL) with free tier. Some community concern after Liquid Web acquisition about product direction and pricing changes. No data collection or privacy issues inherent to the plugin itself.

Kadence WP Kadence.history: Kadence Theme is a lightweight, fast WordPress theme by Kadence WP (Ben Ritner). Available free on WordPress.org with a pro version. Acquired by Liquid Web/iThemes. Popular for performance and full-site editing support in Gutenberg. Developer stepped back late 2024.
Kadence WP Kadence.summary_abuse: No significant abuse or privacy concerns. GPL-licensed WordPress theme. Post-acquisition community concerns about future roadmap, but no active misconduct.

Kadence WP Virtue.history: Virtue is a legacy WordPress theme by Kadence WP (Ben Ritner), originally launched around 2013. It was one of the early popular free themes from the Kadence portfolio before the main Kadence Theme became the flagship. Maintained but no longer the primary product.
Kadence WP Virtue.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Legacy GPL theme that receives maintenance updates. No data collection concerns. Superseded by the newer Kadence Theme as the flagship product.

Kaira Vogue.history: Vogue is a popular multipurpose WordPress theme by Kaira, an Indian theme developer with 32 themes. Available free on WordPress.org and as a premium version. 5/5 stars on WordPress.org. Compatible with Elementor, Divi, and other page builders.
Kaira Vogue.summary_abuse: No abuse history. GPL-licensed WordPress theme from reputable developer Kaira. No data collection or privacy concerns inherent to the theme.

Kaisa.history: Kaisa was founded in 2009 in Uppsala, Sweden as Freespee, a call-tracking and customer experience platform. The company raised venture funding from investors including Verdane, Monterro, Ventech, and Harbert European Growth Capital. In February 2024, Freespee rebranded to Kaisa, pivoting its brand identity to reflect its evolved focus on personalised buyer and seller engagement for automotive OEMs and real estate marketplaces across 52 countries.
Kaisa.summary_abuse: Kaisa is a B2B customer engagement platform targeting automotive and real estate enterprises; it is not accessible to end users or the public. The platform handles contact and behavioural data from buyer journeys, giving it a moderate privacy surface, but there is no documented pattern of abuse. The platform requires enterprise contracts, which means anonymous or low-friction sign-up is not possible. No notable abuse incidents or security research reports have been published.

Kajabi.history: Kajabi was founded in 2010 in Irvine, California by Kenny Rueter and Travis Rosser. It raised $550M in 2021 at a $2B valuation led by Tiger Global. Platform serves over 50,000 entrepreneurs with online courses, communities, and digital products. Reported $100M ARR milestone.
Kajabi.summary_abuse: Kajabi has been criticized for aggressive upsell emails and pricing tier restrictions. Some creators report difficulty canceling subscriptions. No major data breaches or fraud complaints. Platform is used by some questionable info-product sellers but Kajabi itself is legitimate.

Kakao.history: Kakao Corp was co-founded in 2006 by Kim Bum-soo. KakaoTalk launched 2010 and reached 100M users in 2 years, dominating South Korea (90% market share). Merged with Daum Communications in 2014. Diversified into banking, payments, entertainment, mobility. Major KOSPI-listed conglomerate.
Kakao.summary_abuse: Kakao has faced regulatory scrutiny in South Korea over data privacy practices and monopolistic behavior. Kakao Pay faced investigation over user data sharing with China's Alipay in 2022. Korea's personal information protection commission investigated Kakao multiple times.

Kalio.history: Kalio Commerce was founded in 2011 in San Jose, CA by Jonathan Lee (ex-Corio/IBM) and Mike Wachner. Provides SaaS ecommerce for mid-market retailers with $15M ARR and ~51 employees. Targets retailers needing more than SMB platforms but less than enterprise solutions.
Kalio.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Mid-market ecommerce SaaS with PCI-DSS compliance. Small company with no reported incidents.

Kalix.history: Kalix was founded in 2012 as a HIPAA-compliant cloud EMR and practice management platform for dietitians and allied health professionals. Serves 2,500+ practitioners and 800,000+ patients. Affordable entry at $27/month. HIPAA and Australian Privacy Principles compliant.
Kalix.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Healthcare practice management for small providers. HIPAA-compliant. Transparent pricing.

Kaltura.history: Kaltura was founded in 2006 and launched publicly at TechCrunch40 in 2007 in New York. The company built a video content management and streaming platform that became dominant in education and enterprise markets. In July 2021 Kaltura went public on the NASDAQ under ticker KLTR, raising $150 million at a $1.24 billion valuation.
Kaltura.summary_abuse: Kaltura is an enterprise video management and streaming platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves educational institutions and large enterprises under contract, with no free-tier hosting of arbitrary public content, giving fraudsters no useful surface to exploit.

KAMAR.history: KAMAR is a New Zealand student management system designed by NZ teachers for NZ schools. Has been operating for over 25 years. Used by ~340 secondary schools in NZ, representing the majority of secondary schools. Leading SMS in New Zealand education sector.
KAMAR.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Handles student personal data for NZ schools. Complies with NZ Privacy Act. No incidents reported. Well-regarded in NZ education sector.

Kameleoon.history: Kameleoon was founded in 2008 in Paris, France. It provides A/B testing, personalization, and feature flagging. Total funding is approximately $5.65M. Reported $22.1M revenue in 2024. ISO 27001 certified and headquartered in the EU, strong GDPR focus.
Kameleoon.summary_abuse: Kameleoon injects JavaScript onto websites for experimentation and personalization, which can introduce privacy risks if misconfigured. No documented data misuse. Being EU-based and ISO 27001 certified provides some assurance of data handling practices.

Kamozi.history: Kamozi is a small marketing automation SaaS targeting ecommerce brands, focused on brand growth and revenue acceleration. The company appears to be based in Ireland and founded around 2020, but limited public information is available about its origins or ownership.
Kamozi.summary_abuse: Kamozi is a marketing automation platform for ecommerce businesses used to send targeted communications. As with most email/marketing automation tools it can in principle be used for spam, but it is a niche B2B SaaS with no documented abuse history or notable security incidents.

Kamva.history: Kamva was founded in 2016 in Tehran, Iran as a Shopify-like hosted ecommerce platform for Iranian merchants, created to fill the gap left by the absence of international ecommerce SaaS tools in the Iranian market. The platform was built with particular attention to Iran's low average internet speeds and local CDN infrastructure constraints.
Kamva.summary_abuse: Kamva is a hosted ecommerce platform serving Iranian merchants, operating in a market with limited international payment infrastructure. There is no documented abuse history specific to Kamva. The platform enables merchants to create online stores, but it operates in a local Iranian context and does not appear to be exploited for international phishing or scam campaigns.

Kangaroo Rewards.history: Kangaroo Rewards was founded in 2012 in Montreal, Quebec, Canada. Omnichannel loyalty engine serving SMBs and Fortune 500 companies. Integrates with Shopify, Lightspeed, Magento. Offers white-label solutions for businesses globally.
Kangaroo Rewards.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Canadian loyalty/rewards platform. Collects customer purchase and behavior data. No security incidents reported.

Kangle.history: Kangle is a Chinese web server and reverse proxy developed by Kang Hongjiu. Available in open-source and commercial versions. Used mainly in Chinese web hosting as an alternative to Apache/Nginx. Includes an HTTP management console and virtual hosting support.
Kangle.summary_abuse: Chinese-origin web server with limited security audit history. Used mainly in Chinese hosting market where it may be bundled with hosting control panels of uncertain provenance. Chinese-controlled infrastructure warrants caution for Western deployments.

KanzOboz.history: KanzOboz has operated since approximately 2004 as a Russian B2B industry portal for the stationery and office supplies sector, connecting manufacturers, distributors, and buyers. Over time the platform expanded to companion sites including KidsOboz.ru for children's products. It functions primarily as a trade directory and news portal rather than a direct ecommerce retailer.
KanzOboz.summary_abuse: KanzOboz is a niche Russian trade directory for the stationery and office supplies industry with no documented abuse or fraud history. It is a B2B information platform with no consumer-facing self-service commerce features that would attract phishing or scam operators.

Kapa.ai.history: Kapa.ai was co-founded by Emil Sorensen and Finn Bauer, backed by Y Combinator. In October 2024, the company raised $3.2M in seed funding led by Initialized Capital with participation from Y Combinator. Customers include OpenAI, Docker, Monday.com, and Next.js.
Kapa.ai.summary_abuse: Kapa.ai is a B2B AI chatbot platform for technical documentation. SOC 2 Type II certified with PII detection features. No documented abuse incidents. As an AI tool handling technical content, the main risks are misinformation from the AI, which the company addresses through source citation and grounding.

Kapix Studio.history: Kapix Studio is a French no-code platform founded around 2018 that lets users create websites and mobile apps with code export capability. Bridges no-code and traditional development by allowing export to developer repositories. GDPR-compliant by French law.
Kapix Studio.summary_abuse: No abuse history. French no-code builder with GDPR compliance. Small startup with limited public profile.

Kapture CRM.history: Kapture CX (formerly Kapture CRM) was founded in 2014 in Bengaluru, India by Vikas Garg and Sheshgiri Kamath. Raised $11.3M total with Series A led by Cactus Venture Partners. Serves 1,000+ customers in 19 countries including Nykaa, Zepto, and Tata companies.
Kapture CRM.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Indian customer support automation platform. Relatively small scale with reputable enterprise clients.

Karma.history: Karma was created in 2012 by Vojta Jina as part of his university thesis at Google, originally called Testacular before being renamed. It became the default test runner for AngularJS projects and was widely adopted across the JavaScript ecosystem for browser-based unit testing. In January 2023, the Angular team announced Karma would be deprecated in favor of newer testing approaches such as Jest and Web Test Runner, with the project entering maintenance mode.
Karma.summary_abuse: Karma is a developer tool (JavaScript test runner) with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal whatsoever.

Karoo Chat.history: Karoo Chat is a Brazilian live chat platform developed by Alterdata, a Brazilian software company. Supports WhatsApp integration, allows up to 3 simultaneous customers per agent. Primarily targets Brazilian/Portuguese-speaking small businesses.
Karoo Chat.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Small Brazilian live chat tool. Limited to Brazilian market primarily. No international security certifications.

Karte.history: KARTE is a CX platform by PLAID Inc. (Tokyo, Japan), launched March 2015. Raised $25M in April 2018. Analyzes website visitor behavior in real-time and enables personalized communications. ISO 27001 certified. Top choice for Japanese enterprise marketers.
Karte.summary_abuse: KARTE collects detailed individual user behavioral data in real-time. Significant privacy implications when used without proper consent mechanisms. No direct incidents but inherent risks of behavioral tracking at individual level.

Kartify.history: Kartify is an ecommerce platform providing tools for online store creation and management. No notable ownership or deprecation events documented.
Kartify.summary_abuse: Kartify is an ecommerce platform. Like any hosted storefront solution, it could theoretically be used for fake online stores, but no specific abuse incidents have been documented.

Kartmax.history: KartmaX is a headless, serverless ecommerce platform built by GreenHonchos (Noida, India), founded in 2018 by Navin Joshua. Serves 50+ Indian D2C brands including W For Women, Aurelia, Metro Shoes. Uses AI for demand forecasting and service-oriented architecture for scalability.
Kartmax.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Indian D2C ecommerce platform from GreenHonchos. Serves mid-market Indian fashion and retail brands. PCI-DSS compliant.

Kartra.history: Kartra launched in 2018 by Genesis Digital LLC (La Jolla, CA), the same company behind WebinarJam and EverWebinar. It is an all-in-one marketing platform for digital entrepreneurs. Genesis Digital claims over $1.5B in sales facilitated on the platform.
Kartra.summary_abuse: Kartra is marketed heavily to online business owners and info-product creators, a sector associated with aggressive marketing tactics. Some user reviews report difficulty canceling and unexpected billing. No confirmed data breaches. Limited security transparency.

Kartris.history: Kartris is an open-source e-commerce platform built on ASP.NET, originally developed around 2008 in the UK. It was designed for small to medium online retailers as an alternative to commercial carts. The project has seen very little activity in recent years and appears largely dormant, with its GitHub repository showing minimal commits.
Kartris.summary_abuse: Kartris is a self-hosted open-source e-commerce platform with no notable abuse signal. As a downloadable software package, it has no hosted consumer surface. Abuse potential is minimal and limited to misuse by operators of rogue storefronts, which is not specific to Kartris.

Kasada.history: Kasada was founded in 2017 in Sydney, Australia, as a bot detection and mitigation platform focused on defeating advanced automation threats. It raised Series B funding and expanded its US presence. The platform uses a unique approach of making bot attacks economically unviable rather than simply blocking them.
Kasada.summary_abuse: Kasada is a bot detection and cybersecurity platform used to protect websites from automated threats. It has no notable abuse history as an attacker tool. The technology itself is defensive in nature, helping enterprises block credential stuffing, scraping, and fraud bots. No documented cases of misuse or exploitation of the Kasada platform itself.

Kasika.history: Kasika is a French real estate CRM and marketing automation SaaS founded around 2020. It targets real estate agencies with tools for lead management, email campaigns, and customer relationship management. The company is based in France and serves primarily the French real estate market.
Kasika.summary_abuse: Kasika is a CRM and marketing tool for real estate agencies with no notable abuse signal. As a B2B SaaS targeted at professional real estate firms, it has a low-risk profile. No documented misuse patterns specific to this platform.

KaTeX.history: KaTeX was created by Khan Academy and released in 2014, developed by Emily Eisenberg and Sophie Alpert. It became one of the top 5 trending GitHub projects in September 2014. The project provides fast, server-side math rendering using LaTeX syntax and is MIT licensed. Khan Academy maintained the library and it is now community-maintained.
KaTeX.summary_abuse: KaTeX is a JavaScript library for rendering mathematical notation. No consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse patterns. It is a pure rendering library with no network calls or data collection.

Kauz.history: Kauz is a Swiss conversational AI and chatbot platform founded around 2016. It specializes in natural language processing for enterprise customer service applications, particularly in the German-speaking market. The company focuses on building AI-powered dialogue systems for enterprises in Switzerland and Germany.
Kauz.summary_abuse: Kauz is a B2B enterprise chatbot and conversational AI platform with no notable abuse signal. It serves enterprise customers in the DACH region for customer service automation. No documented misuse patterns specific to this platform.

KeaBuilder.history: KeaBuilder is a New Zealand-based website and landing page builder founded around 2015. It targets small businesses and individuals seeking simple drag-and-drop site creation. The product remains a small independent offering serving predominantly local markets.
KeaBuilder.summary_abuse: KeaBuilder is a small website builder with limited consumer-facing hosting. As a page builder platform with user-hosted content, there is some theoretical risk of phishing or scam pages being built, which is common to all website builders. No specific documented abuse incidents. Platform is small enough that systematic abuse would be noticed quickly.

Keap.history: Keap was founded in 2001 by Scott and Eric Martineau as Infusionsoft, a CRM and email marketing platform for small businesses. In January 2019 the company rebranded to Keap. In October 2024, Keap was acquired by Thryv.
Keap.summary_abuse: Keap is a CRM and email marketing platform for small businesses. As an email marketing platform, it has inherent spam potential, but Keap enforces AUP and CAN-SPAM compliance. No specific fraud or scam abuse pattern has been documented.

Keen Delivery.history: Keen Delivery is a last-mile delivery management SaaS platform targeting small to mid-size retailers and e-commerce businesses. Founded around 2019, it focuses on order tracking, route optimization, and delivery logistics. The company appears to operate primarily in France and the broader European market.
Keen Delivery.summary_abuse: Keen Delivery is a delivery logistics SaaS with no notable abuse signal. As a B2B logistics tool, it has minimal consumer-facing abuse surface. No documented misuse patterns.

Keen-Slider.history: Keen-Slider is an open-source JavaScript slider/carousel library created by Roman Beyer, first released around 2020. It gained popularity as a lightweight alternative to heavier carousel libraries like Swiper. The library supports touch gestures, animations, and multiple frameworks including React, Vue, and Angular.
Keen-Slider.summary_abuse: Keen-Slider is a client-side JavaScript UI library with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal. It is a foundational frontend component with no data collection or server-side component.

keep. archeevo.history: keep. archeevo is an archival management software developed by KEEP SOLUTIONS, a Portuguese company founded in 2008 as a spin-off from the University of Minho. It is used primarily by archival institutions in Portugal including government ministries, universities, and municipalities.
keep. archeevo.summary_abuse: keep. archeevo is a specialized archival management system for institutional users with no notable abuse signal. It targets archival institutions and has no consumer-facing surface or features that would attract fraudsters.

Keepeek.history: Keepeek is a French digital asset management (DAM) platform founded around 2008. It specializes in helping organizations manage, store, and distribute digital media assets. The company serves primarily enterprise clients in France and internationally, with a focus on media organizations, agencies, and large corporations.
Keepeek.summary_abuse: Keepeek is an enterprise digital asset management platform with no notable abuse signal. As a B2B DAM solution, it has no consumer-facing hosted content surface. No documented misuse patterns specific to this platform.

KelonCloud.history: KelonCloud is a cloud-based software platform with limited public documentation. It appears to offer ERP or business management solutions primarily targeting Chinese SMEs. Very limited information is available about its founding history or corporate structure.
KelonCloud.summary_abuse: KelonCloud is a cloud business software platform with minimal public presence. No documented abuse signal specific to this platform. Limited information is available to assess its abuse posture.

Kemal.history: Kemal is an open-source web framework written in Crystal, created by Serdar Doğruyol in Turkey and first released in 2015. It draws inspiration from Sinatra (Ruby) and provides a simple, fast, and expressive framework for building web applications. Crystal's performance characteristics made Kemal popular as a fast alternative to interpreted-language frameworks.
Kemal.summary_abuse: Kemal is a server-side web framework with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal. It is a backend infrastructure tool used by developers to build web applications. No misuse patterns specific to Kemal.

Kendo UI.history: Kendo UI was created by Telerik, a Bulgarian software company founded in 2002, and released around 2011 as an HTML5 and jQuery UI component library. In 2014 Telerik open-sourced Kendo UI Core under the Apache 2.0 license. Progress Software acquired Telerik in October 2014 for $262.5 million, making Kendo UI part of the Progress product portfolio.
Kendo UI.summary_abuse: Kendo UI is a UI component library with no notable abuse signal. It is a frontend framework used to build web applications and has no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters would target.

Kenect.history: Kenect is a US-based customer communication and text messaging platform founded around 2015, primarily targeting automotive dealers, home services, and healthcare businesses. It focuses on enabling businesses to communicate with customers via text message. The company has raised venture capital funding.
Kenect.summary_abuse: Kenect is a text messaging and customer communication SaaS platform. Like all bulk SMS platforms, there is a theoretical risk of misuse for spam or phishing text messages, but this is common to all SMS marketing platforms. Kenect primarily serves legitimate businesses in regulated industries. No documented notable abuse incidents specific to Kenect.

Kenlo.history: Kenlo is a Brazilian real estate management SaaS platform founded around 2014. It provides property management, CRM, and sales tools primarily for Brazilian real estate agencies and developers. The company serves the Brazilian market and has received investment funding.
Kenlo.summary_abuse: Kenlo is a B2B real estate management SaaS platform serving the Brazilian market. No notable abuse signal. As a professional tool for real estate companies, it has a low abuse risk profile.

Kentico CMS.history: Kentico CMS was founded in 2004 in Brno, Czech Republic by Petr Palas. It is one of the leading .NET-based content management systems and has evolved into a broader digital experience platform. In 2019, Kentico launched Kentico Kontent (now Kontent.ai) as a headless CMS product alongside its traditional Kentico CMS, allowing two separate product lines. The company remains privately held and headquartered in the Czech Republic.
Kentico CMS.summary_abuse: Kentico CMS is an enterprise content management platform with no notable direct abuse signal. As a self-hosted and cloud-hosted CMS, it could theoretically host malicious content, but this is common to all CMS platforms. The platform is primarily used by large enterprises and has active security monitoring. CVE disclosures have been addressed promptly historically.

Keptify.history: Keptify is a UK-based e-commerce exit intent and cart abandonment solution for online retailers. It focuses on capturing leads from visitors who are about to leave an online store. Founded around 2016, it serves primarily European small and medium e-commerce businesses.
Keptify.summary_abuse: Keptify is an e-commerce lead capture tool with limited abuse potential. It operates as a JavaScript widget embedded in retailer sites. No documented abuse patterns specific to this platform.

Kerberos.history: Kerberos is a network authentication protocol originally developed at MIT as part of Project Athena in 1987. Version 5, the current widely-deployed version, was published as RFC 1510 in 1993 (updated by RFC 4120 in 2005). Kerberos is the default authentication protocol for Windows Active Directory, macOS, and many Unix/Linux environments. It has become foundational internet infrastructure used by virtually every enterprise network worldwide.
Kerberos.summary_abuse: Kerberos is a foundational authentication protocol used by virtually every enterprise network. It has no consumer-facing surface and no documented direct abuse. Attacks against Kerberos (such as Pass-the-Ticket or Kerberoasting) target implementations and configuration weaknesses rather than the protocol itself. These are defensive concerns for system administrators, not vendor-side issues.

KerningJS.history: KerningJS is a small open-source JavaScript library for improving CSS kerning and letter-spacing on web pages. It was created around 2012 and appears to have been abandoned with no recent activity.
KerningJS.summary_abuse: KerningJS is a tiny abandoned CSS kerning JavaScript library with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal.

Kestrel.history: Kestrel is the cross-platform web server built into ASP.NET Core, first introduced by Microsoft in 2016 alongside ASP.NET Core 1.0. It replaced the Windows-only HttpListener and IIS integration as the default server for .NET web apps. Kestrel is open-source under the MIT license and maintained by Microsoft as part of the ASP.NET Core project on GitHub.
Kestrel.summary_abuse: Kestrel is a web server component built into ASP.NET Core with no consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal. It is foundational server infrastructure maintained by Microsoft. No misuse patterns apply to Kestrel itself.

Ketch.history: Ketch is a data privacy and compliance automation platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in San Francisco. It helps enterprises manage consent, data subject requests, and regulatory compliance across GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy laws. The company has raised significant VC funding and serves large enterprise clients.
Ketch.summary_abuse: Ketch is a privacy and compliance platform designed to help businesses manage user consent and data privacy. It has no notable abuse signal and is itself a tool for improving data privacy compliance. The platform is used by enterprises to meet GDPR, CCPA, and similar regulatory requirements.

Kevel.history: Kevel (formerly Adzerk) was founded in 2010 and rebranded to Kevel in 2021 to better reflect its API-first ad server positioning. It provides a developer-focused ad infrastructure API that allows companies to build custom native advertising, sponsored listings, and internal ad networks. The company is headquartered in Durham, NC and has raised VC funding.
Kevel.summary_abuse: Kevel is an ad server API platform used by enterprises to build custom ad networks. As an ad-tech platform, it facilitates targeted advertising which carries some inherent privacy concerns, but it operates B2B rather than having a direct consumer surface. The platform could theoretically be used to serve deceptive ads if customers misuse it, but Kevel has acceptable use policies.

Keybase.history: Keybase was founded in 2014 by Chris Coyne and Max Krohn to create a public key infrastructure system for verifying online identities. The platform combined cryptographic identity verification with end-to-end encrypted messaging and file storage. In May 2020, Keybase was acquired by Zoom Video Communications to improve Zoom's security. Development has slowed since the acquisition but the service remains operational.
Keybase.summary_abuse: Keybase is an encrypted messaging and identity verification platform. Like any encrypted communication service, it can be used for private communication by both legitimate users and bad actors. As an end-to-end encrypted platform, Keybase has limited ability to monitor message content. No major documented abuse incidents, though the encrypted messaging space generally presents challenges for law enforcement monitoring.

KeyCDN.history: KeyCDN is a content delivery network founded in 2013 in Switzerland. It offers HTTP/2 CDN services at competitive pricing and has grown to serve customers globally. The company remains independently owned and operated without VC funding, which distinguishes it from major CDN providers.
KeyCDN.summary_abuse: KeyCDN is a content delivery network that can be used to distribute content globally. Like all CDN providers, it can be misused to serve malicious content, phishing pages, or malware. The company has abuse reporting mechanisms and maintains an acceptable use policy. Abuse risk is moderate, consistent with CDN providers of this size.

Keymailer.history: Keymailer is a UK-based platform founded around 2015 that connects game developers with streamers and content creators for game key distribution and influencer marketing. It serves the video gaming industry by enabling indie developers to send game keys to content creators for review and promotion.
Keymailer.summary_abuse: Keymailer is a game key distribution platform for streamers and content creators. There is some potential for misuse through fake accounts claiming game keys without creating content, but this is platform-specific fraud rather than broader societal harm. Keymailer has verification processes for creators. No major documented abuse incidents.

KeyReply.history: KeyReply is a Singapore-based conversational AI and chatbot platform founded in 2016. It focuses on enterprise customer service automation, particularly in the healthcare and government sectors across Southeast Asia. The company has received funding and serves enterprise clients in Singapore and the broader APAC region.
KeyReply.summary_abuse: KeyReply is a B2B enterprise chatbot platform serving healthcare and government sectors with no notable abuse signal. The platform is primarily used by legitimate enterprises for customer service automation. No documented misuse patterns.

Keyvos.history: Keyvos is a Greek ecommerce SaaS platform developed by INFOCUBE, a software company based in Athens, Greece. It is built on a Laravel/MySQL/Elasticsearch stack and targets Greek SME merchants with hosted storefront customization, product and order management, SEO tools, and marketplace integrations. The platform participates in Greece's NextGeneration EU digital support programmes.
Keyvos.summary_abuse: Keyvos is a niche Greek ecommerce platform serving local SME merchants. There is no documented abuse history. The platform enables merchants to set up online stores, which in general carries low-level abuse risk, but Keyvos's small scale and local Greek market focus means it is not a known target for organized phishing or scam infrastructure.

Keywee.history: Keywee is a content distribution and audience intelligence platform founded in 2014, helping publishers grow paid subscriptions and advertising revenue via AI-driven targeting on Facebook and other social platforms. Raised several rounds of VC funding.
Keywee.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Keywee is a B2B SaaS tool used by media publishers for audience development. It does not offer consumer-facing hosting or free-tier services that would attract abusers. Standard ad-tech data collection practices apply.

Kibana.history: Kibana was created by Rashid Khan and released in 2013 as an open-source visualization tool for Elasticsearch. Elastic (formerly Elasticsearch BV) acquired it and made it the official frontend for the Elastic Stack (ELK Stack). In 2021, Elastic relicensed Kibana from Apache 2.0 to Elastic License 2.0 and SSPL, controversially restricting cloud provider usage, prompting AWS to fork it as OpenSearch Dashboards.
Kibana.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Kibana is infrastructure/analytics tooling used by enterprises and ops teams. It has no consumer-facing surface and no free hosting features that would attract abusers. The relicensing controversy in 2021 was a business decision, not a security or abuse issue.

Kibo Commerce.history: Kibo Commerce was formed in 2016 when Vista Equity Partners consolidated three ecommerce companies (Fiverun, MarketLive, and Shopatron) into a single platform. It has since acquired Monetate and Certona to expand its personalization capabilities.
Kibo Commerce.summary_abuse: Kibo Commerce is an enterprise-grade ecommerce platform with sales-gated pricing targeting large retailers. It has no notable abuse signal and serves established brands rather than anonymous users.

Kibo Personalization.history: Kibo Personalization originated from Certona, a personalization engine founded in 2004, and Monetate, founded in 2008. Both were acquired by Kibo Commerce and integrated into its unified commerce platform under the Kibo Personalization brand by 2019.
Kibo Personalization.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kibo Personalization is a B2B enterprise personalization engine delivered exclusively to vetted retailers. Enterprise-only pricing and onboarding makes abuse highly unlikely.

Kickflip.history: Kickflip is a product customization SaaS platform founded in Canada around 2012, enabling e-commerce merchants to offer visual product configurators. It targets niche product customization use cases like apparel and accessories.
Kickflip.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kickflip is a niche B2B product customization tool with limited consumer exposure. Not a vector for common abuse patterns.

Kicksite.history: Kicksite is a martial arts school management SaaS platform founded in 2008, offering billing, attendance, and student management tools specifically for martial arts studios. It has remained an independent niche product serving a specific vertical.
Kicksite.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kicksite is a niche vertical SaaS for martial arts studios with a limited consumer surface and subscription model that requires legitimate business setup.

Kilatech.history: Kilatech is a small African tech startup offering web and digital marketing services. Minimal public history available; appears to be a regional digital agency or SaaS provider operating primarily in Nigeria.
Kilatech.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on Kilatech. Small regional provider with minimal web presence. No documented abuse incidents, but limited transparency about operations and data practices.

Kiliba.history: Kiliba is a French AI-powered email marketing automation platform founded in 2020, specifically designed for e-commerce businesses. It uses machine learning to automatically send personalized emails at optimal times without requiring manual campaign setup.
Kiliba.summary_abuse: Kiliba is an email marketing platform and carries the inherent abuse risk of bulk email tools, including potential use for sending spam or phishing if misused. However, it targets legitimate e-commerce clients and does not offer free tiers. GDPR-compliant as a French company, suggesting attention to data practices.

Kilkaya.history: Kilkaya is a Danish web analytics and audience measurement platform founded around 2010, serving Nordic media companies and publishers. It competes with tools like Google Analytics in the Scandinavian market.
Kilkaya.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kilkaya is a B2B analytics platform for publishers. As a web analytics tool it collects visitor data, but its primary use is legitimate audience measurement for media clients.

Kimonix.history: Kimonix is an Israeli AI-powered product merchandising platform founded in 2020, focused on helping Shopify merchants optimize product sorting and collection pages to increase conversion rates.
Kimonix.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kimonix is a B2B Shopify app focused on product merchandising for legitimate e-commerce merchants.

Kindful.history: Kindful is a cloud-based donor management and fundraising platform for nonprofits. It was acquired by Bloomerang in January 2021 in a deal combining two major donor management platforms.
Kindful.summary_abuse: Kindful is a nonprofit donor management platform with no notable abuse signal. It handles fundraising data for legitimate nonprofits and has no consumer-facing surface targeted by scammers.

KineticJS.history: KineticJS was a JavaScript HTML5 canvas library created by Eric Rowell around 2011, used for building interactive canvas applications. It was officially deprecated in 2015 when development halted; the project was forked as Konva.js, which continues as the active successor.
KineticJS.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. KineticJS is an abandoned frontend canvas library with no server-side surface. It has been deprecated since 2015 in favor of Konva.js.

Kinsta.history: Kinsta is a managed WordPress and application hosting provider founded in 2013, built on Google Cloud Platform. It has grown to become one of the top managed WordPress hosts, known for premium performance and developer-friendly tooling. In 2024, Kinsta expanded beyond WordPress to offer application and database hosting.
Kinsta.summary_abuse: Kinsta, as a web hosting provider, has some exposure to hosted content abuse (malware, phishing sites). However, Kinsta's premium pricing and vetting process significantly reduces abuse compared to cheaper shared hosting alternatives. The company maintains active security monitoring and responds to abuse reports.

Kintone.history: Kintone is a no-code business application platform developed by Japanese software company Cybozu and launched internationally around 2011. Cybozu, founded in 1997, is a publicly traded Japanese software company known for collaboration tools. Kintone is its flagship international product for workflow automation.
Kintone.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kintone is an enterprise workflow automation platform with business verification at signup. The platform is not typically exploited for common abuse patterns.

Kiosked.history: Kiosked is a Finnish ad-tech company founded in 2011 that provides contextual advertising and monetization solutions for publishers. It has operated in the programmatic advertising space, focusing on native and contextual ad formats.
Kiosked.summary_abuse: Kiosked is an ad-tech platform with typical ad-tech abuse exposure. Ad networks are sometimes used to distribute malvertising. As a smaller ad tech player, enforcement and takedown responsiveness may be limited compared to major networks.

Kiprotect.history: KIProtect is a German privacy technology company founded in 2018, developing tools for GDPR-compliant data processing, including the open-source Klaro consent manager. The company focuses on data anonymization and privacy engineering services.
Kiprotect.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. KIProtect is a privacy-focused German company that builds consent management and data anonymization tools. Its mission is explicitly pro-privacy and GDPR compliance, making it an unlikely abuse vector.

Kirby.history: Kirby is a file-based CMS created by German developer Bastian Allgeier, first released in 2012. It uses no database, storing content in files. Kirby has maintained a loyal developer community and has been developed independently without VC backing. It uses a paid per-site license model.
Kirby.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kirby is a self-hosted file-based CMS used by developers. The paid license model and developer-centric nature make it unsuitable for bulk abuse operations. Security vulnerabilities are actively patched.

Kirki Customizer Framework.history: Kirki Customizer Framework is a WordPress plugin and PHP library developed by Aristeides Stathopoulos, first released around 2014. It provides an enhanced API for building WordPress Customizer options, making it easier for theme developers to add customization settings.
Kirki Customizer Framework.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Kirki is a WordPress developer tool for building theme customizers. It has no consumer-facing surface and is not associated with any abuse patterns.

KISSmetrics.history: KISSmetrics was founded in 2008 by Neil Patel and Hiten Shah as a customer analytics platform. Neil Patel later departed from day-to-day involvement. The company remains active and acquired Sandstorm Analytics in 2025 to expand into gaming analytics.
KISSmetrics.summary_abuse: KISSmetrics is a behavioral analytics platform that tracks individual user actions. As marketing analytics, it profiles user behavior, which raises privacy concerns but is not associated with scam or fraud abuse patterns.

Kit.history: Kit (formerly ConvertKit) is an email marketing platform for creators, founded by Nathan Barry in 2013. It became one of the leading email platforms for bloggers, YouTubers, and creators. In 2024, ConvertKit rebranded to Kit to reflect its broader creator commerce vision.
Kit.summary_abuse: Kit (ConvertKit) is an email marketing platform and has inherent risk of misuse for spam. The company has anti-spam policies and monitors for abuse, but free-tier email platforms are sometimes used for cold outreach spam. Overall, Kit has a good reputation in the creator community with responsive abuse enforcement.

KIT CMS.history: KIT CMS is a .NET-based content management system developed by Kontrolit.net, a UK web agency incorporated on 10 August 1998 in Yeovil, Somerset, England. The KIT CMS was first built in 2001 and has undergone over two decades of continuous development, primarily serving SME clients in the UK market alongside Kontrolit's web design and digital marketing services.
KIT CMS.summary_abuse: KIT CMS is a proprietary .NET CMS used exclusively by clients of the Yeovil-based UK web agency Kontrolit. It has no notable abuse signal; it is a managed product deployed for small UK businesses, with no self-service or anonymous access features that would make it a target for phishing or spam campaigns.

Kitcart.history: Kitcart is a Nigerian e-commerce platform founded around 2020, targeting African merchants with tools to set up online stores. It is a regional player in the growing African e-commerce infrastructure space.
Kitcart.summary_abuse: Limited public data on Kitcart. As a free-tier e-commerce platform, it carries some exposure to fake storefront creation for fraud. Limited transparency about anti-abuse measures.

Kites.history: Kites is a small business messaging and team communication tool. Very limited public history available; appears to be a startup with minimal public presence.
Kites.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on Kites. Small team communication tool with minimal public presence. No documented abuse incidents but limited transparency.

Kiva.history: Kiva is a US nonprofit microlending platform founded in 2005 by Matt Flannery and Jessica Jackley in San Francisco. It pioneered online peer-to-peer microlending to entrepreneurs in developing countries. Over $1.8 billion has been loaned through the platform.
Kiva.summary_abuse: Kiva has historically had some fraud exposure related to false loan applications and misrepresentation by field partners, which the organization has worked to address through vetting processes. Overall it is a well-established nonprofit with strong community trust and transparent operations.

Kiwi Sizing.history: Kiwi Sizing is a Canadian Shopify app founded around 2017 that provides AI-powered size recommendation tools for fashion e-commerce stores, helping customers find the right clothing size to reduce returns.
Kiwi Sizing.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kiwi Sizing is a niche B2B Shopify app for size recommendations. Not a likely abuse vector.

Kiwify.history: Kiwify is a Brazilian digital product marketplace and platform founded around 2020, enabling creators to sell online courses, ebooks, and memberships. It has grown rapidly in the Brazilian creator economy market, competing with Hotmart and Eduzz.
Kiwify.summary_abuse: Kiwify, as a digital product marketplace, has exposure to fraudulent digital products and scam courses. Brazilian digital product platforms have historically been associated with aggressive affiliate marketing and some low-quality or misleading products. No specific major fraud incidents documented but the free-tier model and consumer-facing nature elevate risk.

Kizen.history: Kizen is a US-based automation and AI personalization SaaS targeting healthcare, insurance, and financial services industries. No notable acquisition or ownership events documented.
Kizen.summary_abuse: Kizen is an enterprise marketing automation and AI personalization platform with no notable abuse signal. It operates in regulated industries with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically exploit.

KKTIX.history: KKTIX is Taiwan's leading ticketing platform operated by Walking Internet Entertainment Limited. Founded around 2012. Covers concerts, art, conferences, and cultural events in Taiwan. In 2020 launched KKTIX Live for online streaming. Leading ticketing service for popular events in Taiwan.
KKTIX.summary_abuse: No abuse history. Taiwan's dominant event ticketing platform. PCI-DSS compliant for payment processing. Primarily serves Taiwan and nearby markets.

Klangoo.history: Klangoo is a Lebanese AI-powered content recommendation and natural language processing platform founded around 2012. It provides content tagging, semantic analysis, and recommendation APIs to media companies and publishers.
Klangoo.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Klangoo is a B2B NLP and content recommendation API for publishers. Not a consumer-facing platform and not associated with abuse patterns.

Klara.history: Klara is a US patient communication platform founded in 2013, providing HIPAA-compliant messaging for healthcare practices. It enables two-way communication between patients and medical providers via secure messaging and telemedicine features.
Klara.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Klara operates in the healthcare space and is HIPAA-compliant, with strong data protection requirements. Its B2B-only model and healthcare focus make it a low-risk platform for typical abuse vectors.

Klarna Checkout.history: Klarna was founded in Stockholm in 2005 by Sebastian Siemiatkowski, Niklas Adalberth, and Victor Jacobsson as a buy-now-pay-later and payment solutions provider. It became Europe's most valuable fintech unicorn. In 2024, Klarna filed for an IPO on the NYSE. Klarna Checkout is the embedded checkout product that merchants integrate to accept payments.
Klarna Checkout.summary_abuse: Klarna has faced scrutiny from consumer advocates and regulators regarding its BNPL products promoting overspending and debt. The FCA and European regulators have investigated its practices. Klarna Checkout itself has been used in fraudulent transactions where stolen credentials are used to make purchases. Klarna does maintain fraud detection systems and has a responsible disclosure program.

Klarna Virtual Shopping.history: Klarna Virtual Shopping is a product feature launched by Klarna in 2021 that enables social and live shopping experiences within the Klarna app and partner merchant sites. It connects shoppers with store associates for personalized video shopping sessions.
Klarna Virtual Shopping.summary_abuse: No unique abuse signals beyond the Klarna parent platform. The virtual shopping feature connects consumers with sales associates in video sessions, which carries minimal abuse risk at the feature level.

Klaro.history: Klaro is an open-source GDPR consent management tool developed by KIProtect (Germany) and first released in 2019. It provides a configurable cookie consent banner and consent manager for websites, with the core library available under AGPL-3.0.
Klaro.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Klaro is a privacy-focused consent management tool explicitly designed for GDPR compliance. It is not associated with any abuse patterns.

Klasha.history: Klasha is a Nigerian cross-border payments fintech founded in 2021, enabling African businesses to accept and send international payments. It raised seed funding and has expanded to support multiple African currencies.
Klasha.summary_abuse: Klasha is a payments platform and has inherent risk of being used for payment fraud or money laundering. As an African payments startup with limited regulatory maturity, it may have less sophisticated fraud detection than established payment processors. No specific major fraud incidents publicly documented.

Klaviyo.history: Klaviyo was founded in 2013 by Andrew Bialecki and Ed Hallen in Boston, Massachusetts, as an email and SMS marketing platform focused on ecommerce. The company grew to become the dominant email marketing platform for Shopify merchants. In September 2023 Klaviyo went public on the New York Stock Exchange under ticker KVYO, raising $576 million at a $9.2 billion valuation.
Klaviyo.summary_abuse: Klaviyo's email and SMS sending infrastructure is occasionally used for spam and phishing by accounts that pass initial verification. Being the dominant email marketing platform for ecommerce, the volume of legitimate bulk sending coexists with a documented minority of abusive accounts. Klaviyo enforces email compliance policies, monitors for spam reports, and suspends violating accounts, representing a moderate-but-managed abuse surface typical of large marketing email platforms.

Klaviyo Customer Hub.history: Klaviyo Customer Hub is a product feature launched by Klaviyo around 2023, providing a branded customer portal for e-commerce shoppers to manage their subscriptions, orders, and loyalty points. It is part of Klaviyo's broader platform expansion beyond email marketing.
Klaviyo Customer Hub.summary_abuse: No unique abuse signals beyond the Klaviyo parent platform. Customer Hub is a customer portal feature for e-commerce, not a standalone product with distinct abuse vectors.

Klaviyo Data Platform.history: Klaviyo Data Platform (also called Klaviyo CDP) is a customer data platform feature launched by Klaviyo around 2022 as part of its expansion beyond email marketing. It unifies customer data from multiple sources to enable more sophisticated segmentation and personalization.
Klaviyo Data Platform.summary_abuse: No unique abuse signals beyond the Klaviyo parent platform. As a CDP product handling large customer datasets, the main concern is data privacy rather than direct abuse. Klaviyo's compliance certifications mitigate key data risks.

Klaviyo Forms.history: Klaviyo Forms is a built-in feature of the Klaviyo platform, enabling merchants to create and embed sign-up forms, pop-ups, and fly-outs to capture email and SMS subscribers. It has been part of the Klaviyo platform since its early days.
Klaviyo Forms.summary_abuse: No unique abuse signals beyond the Klaviyo parent platform. Sign-up forms embedded by Klaviyo users may collect consumer data; the inherent risk is in how merchants use collected data rather than in the form tool itself.

Klaviyo Reviews.history: Klaviyo Reviews is a product reviews feature launched by Klaviyo in 2023 as part of its broader commerce platform expansion, enabling merchants to collect and display product reviews integrated with their email/SMS marketing workflows.
Klaviyo Reviews.summary_abuse: No unique abuse signals beyond the Klaviyo parent platform. Reviews features can be manipulated with fake reviews, though Klaviyo's integration with verified purchase data reduces this risk versus anonymous review platforms.

Kleecks.history: Kleecks is an Italian SEO and web performance optimization platform founded around 2015, offering server-side rendering and SEO automation tools for enterprise e-commerce websites.
Kleecks.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kleecks is a niche B2B SEO platform for enterprise clients. Not a consumer-facing platform with common abuse vectors.

Kleeja.history: Kleeja is an open-source Arabic file-sharing and upload script, developed around 2007, primarily used in the Arab world to set up file-hosting websites. It enables users to create their own file upload and sharing portals.
Kleeja.summary_abuse: Kleeja is an open-source file hosting script historically used to set up file-sharing sites in the Arab world. Sites built on Kleeja have been used to host pirated software, copyrighted media, and malware. Minimal active maintenance increases abuse risk from unpatched vulnerabilities.

Kleer.history: Kleer is an Argentine agile training and community platform founded around 2015, focused on coaching and consulting for organizations adopting agile methodologies in Latin America.
Kleer.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Kleer is a training and consultancy platform with limited consumer surface. Not a typical abuse vector.

Klevu.history: Klevu is a Finnish AI-powered product discovery and search platform founded in 2013. It provides intelligent site search and product recommendations for e-commerce retailers, with deep integrations for Shopify, Magento, and BigCommerce.
Klevu.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Klevu is a B2B e-commerce search platform with no consumer-facing surface and subscription access that requires business verification.

Klickly.history: Klickly is a US performance commerce platform founded in 2014, enabling direct-to-consumer brands to sell products directly within ad units across publisher sites. It provides a distributed checkout experience embedded in digital advertising.
Klickly.summary_abuse: Klickly embeds purchase flows into ad units, raising some concern about misleading ad-based checkout experiences. As an ad-commerce platform, it has moderate risk of being used to push deceptive or misleading product advertisements.

KlickPages.history: KlickPages is a Brazilian landing page builder and conversion optimization platform founded in 2014 as part of the Klick Group ecosystem in Brazil. It has grown in the Brazilian digital marketing market, catering primarily to Brazilian online marketers.
KlickPages.summary_abuse: KlickPages is a landing page builder with inherent risk of misuse for phishing pages or scam funnels. Brazilian digital marketing platforms have historically shown higher rates of aggressive affiliate marketing and misleading offers. No specific major abuse incidents publicly documented.

KlickTipp.history: KlickTipp is a German email marketing automation platform founded in 2009, targeting DACH (Germany, Austria, Switzerland) market. It emphasizes GDPR compliance as a German company and positions itself as a privacy-first alternative to US email marketing tools.
KlickTipp.summary_abuse: KlickTipp is an email marketing platform with standard email abuse risk (spam potential). Being a German company, it adheres strictly to GDPR and German data protection law, and explicitly markets its GDPR compliance. Lower abuse risk than global mass-market alternatives.

Kliken.history: Kliken is a US digital advertising and Google Shopping automation platform founded around 2008. It provides advertising management tools for small e-commerce merchants, automating Google Shopping and display ad campaigns.
Kliken.summary_abuse: Kliken manages paid advertising campaigns which carries some risk of being used to drive traffic to scam storefronts. As a paid ad-management tool for small businesses, it has lower abuse exposure than consumer-facing free hosting platforms.

Klip.history: Klipfolio (known for its Klip product) is a Canadian business intelligence and dashboard platform founded in 2001. Originally named Serence, it rebranded to Klipfolio in 2011. It provides real-time KPI dashboards for businesses of all sizes and has been bootstrapped for most of its history.
Klip.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Klipfolio is a B2B analytics/dashboard tool with subscription pricing. Not a platform associated with typical abuse vectors.

Klook.history: Klook is a Hong Kong-based online travel activities and experiences booking platform founded in 2014 by Ethan Lin and Eric Gnock Fah. It has grown to become one of Asia's leading travel experience marketplaces, raising over $600M in funding. Impacted significantly by COVID-19 but recovered post-pandemic.
Klook.summary_abuse: Klook as a consumer travel marketplace has had reports of fraudulent activity listings, fake reviews, and refund disputes. It carries typical marketplace abuse risks including fraudulent merchant accounts and counterfeit experience listings. The company moderates content but enforcement quality varies by region.

KMK.history: KMK Soft Solutions was founded in 1998, originally in Turkey, and has international offices in Cyprus. Provides AI SaaS platforms, ecommerce infrastructure, and custom software. 25+ years of experience serving global clients.
KMK.summary_abuse: Very limited information. Turkish-origin software company with Cyprus international entity. No incidents found. Unclear exactly what the tracked technology represents.

Knack.history: Knack is a US no-code database and application builder founded in 2010 by Brian Luerssen. It enables non-technical users to build custom web apps and databases without coding. It has remained bootstrapped and profitable.
Knack.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Knack is a B2B no-code app builder used by organizations for internal tools and databases. Not a typical abuse vector.

Knak.history: Knak is a Canadian email and landing page creation platform founded in 2015, designed for enterprise marketing teams using Marketo, HubSpot, Eloqua, and similar marketing automation platforms. It allows non-technical marketers to build complex email templates.
Knak.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Knak is an enterprise email template builder with sales-gated access. Not a typical abuse vector.

Knit pay.history: Knit Pay is an Indian WordPress payment gateway plugin, first released around 2020. It integrates various Indian payment gateways with WordPress-based membership and event management plugins, primarily targeting Indian e-commerce users.
Knit pay.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Knit Pay is a niche WordPress payment plugin for Indian payment gateways. As a payment plugin, security vulnerabilities could be exploited but no notable incidents documented.

Knock.history: Knock is a US notification infrastructure platform founded in 2021, providing APIs for in-app notifications, email, SMS, and push notifications for developer teams. It raised $10M in Series A funding.
Knock.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Knock is a developer-facing notification infrastructure API. Not a consumer-facing platform with typical abuse patterns.

Knock Knock App.history: Knock Knock App appears to be a small consumer-facing mobile or web application. Very limited public history is available. Likely a solo or tiny team project with minimal public footprint.
Knock Knock App.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on Knock Knock App. Small consumer app with minimal public information. No documented abuse incidents.

Knockout.js.history: Knockout.js is a JavaScript MVVM framework created by Steve Sanderson at Microsoft and open-sourced in 2010. It popularized declarative data binding for web applications. While newer frameworks like React and Vue have taken over, Knockout remains widely deployed in enterprise legacy systems and still receives maintenance updates.
Knockout.js.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Knockout.js is a client-side JavaScript framework with no server-side component or hosting surface. It is not associated with any abuse patterns.

KnoCommerce.history: KnoCommerce is a US post-purchase survey and attribution platform founded around 2018, helping e-commerce brands understand customer acquisition attribution by asking customers how they discovered the brand.
KnoCommerce.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. KnoCommerce is a B2B survey and analytics tool for e-commerce. Not a consumer-facing platform with common abuse vectors.

Knoma.history: Knoma is a UK fintech startup founded around 2020, offering income share agreements and buy-now-pay-later financing for online education and training courses. It allows learners to pay for courses after getting a job.
Knoma.summary_abuse: Knoma operates in the income share agreement space, which has regulatory risk and consumer debt concerns. BNPL-style financing for education has drawn regulatory attention in the UK. Some risk of consumers taking on unaffordable debt obligations.

Knorish.history: Knorish is an Indian online course creation and knowledge commerce platform founded around 2017, targeting coaches, trainers, and educators in India who want to sell online courses and digital products.
Knorish.summary_abuse: Knorish is an online course platform with inherent risk of fraudulent or low-quality course listings. As an Indian platform with a free tier, it has some exposure to scam course operators. No specific major incidents documented.

Ko-fi.history: Ko-fi is a UK-based creator monetization platform founded in 2012 by Simon Ellington. It allows creators to receive tips and donations from fans, and later expanded to memberships, shop features, and commissions. Ko-fi charges no platform fee on the free tier, distinguishing it from Patreon.
Ko-fi.summary_abuse: Ko-fi has exposure to fraudulent fundraising and impersonation of creators. Some creators have reported fraudulent chargebacks after receiving payments. Ko-fi has policies against scams and impersonation but as a consumer-facing donation platform, some risk is inherent. Overall reputation is positive in the creator community.

Koa.history: Koa.js is a Node.js web framework created by the team behind Express.js (including TJ Holowaychuk) and first released in 2013. It was designed as a smaller, more expressive successor to Express, leveraging async/await from the start. It has remained a popular choice for Node.js API development.
Koa.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Koa is a server-side Node.js framework with no hosting surface. It is not associated with any abuse patterns.

Koala.history: Koala is a US B2B intent data and sales intelligence platform founded in 2021. It tracks website visitor intent signals to help sales teams identify prospects ready to buy. It raised $10M in seed funding.
Koala.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Koala is a B2B sales intelligence tool that tracks visitor behavior for B2B sales teams. Its use of visitor tracking raises privacy questions but the tool targets legitimate business use.

Koala Framework.history: Koala Framework is an abandoned PHP framework created around 2010. It has minimal documentation and activity, suggesting it was a small experimental project that was never widely adopted.
Koala Framework.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Koala Framework is an obscure abandoned PHP framework with no hosting surface and no known abuse patterns.

Kobalte.history: Kobalte is an open-source headless UI component library for SolidJS, first released in 2022. It provides accessible, unstyled primitives for building design systems, similar to Radix UI but designed specifically for the SolidJS ecosystem.
Kobalte.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Kobalte is a UI component library for SolidJS with no server-side component or hosting surface.

KobiMaster.history: KobiMaster appears to be a small Israeli SaaS platform with limited public history. Minimal public information available about the company or its products.
KobiMaster.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on KobiMaster. Small SaaS provider with minimal public presence. No documented abuse incidents.

Kochava.history: Kochava is a US mobile measurement and attribution platform founded in 2011, providing app analytics, attribution, and audience data for mobile advertisers. In 2023, the FTC filed a lawsuit alleging Kochava sold precise geolocation data about people visiting sensitive locations including reproductive health clinics and places of worship.
Kochava.summary_abuse: Kochava was sued by the FTC in 2023 for selling precise geolocation data about consumers visiting sensitive locations. The FTC alleged this data could be used to track visits to reproductive health clinics, places of worship, and addiction treatment centers. This is a significant privacy and abuse concern. Kochava denies the allegations and the case is ongoing.

Koha.history: Koha is an open-source integrated library system (ILS) created in New Zealand in 1999 by Katipo Communications for the Horowhenua Library Trust, making it the world's first free and open-source ILS. It is now used by thousands of libraries worldwide and maintained by a global community of contributors.
Koha.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Koha is a library management system deployed by libraries worldwide. It has no consumer-facing hosting surface and is not associated with any abuse patterns.

Kohana.history: Kohana is a PHP framework that originated as a fork of CodeIgniter in 2007, created by community members who wanted cleaner code and better PHP 5 support. It was popular in the late 2000s but development was abandoned around 2016 and the framework is now unmaintained.
Kohana.summary_abuse: No direct abuse signal, but Kohana is an abandoned PHP framework with known security vulnerabilities that remain unpatched since ~2016. Sites still running Kohana are at elevated risk of exploitation due to unpatched CVEs.

Koishi.js.history: Koishi.js is a Chinese open-source chatbot framework for building bots on messaging platforms like Discord, Telegram, QQ, and others. It was first released around 2020 and is primarily popular in the Chinese developer community.
Koishi.js.summary_abuse: Koishi.js is a chatbot framework, which carries some risk of being used to build spam bots or automated abuse tools. As a self-hosted framework with legitimate use cases, it is not inherently abusive but could be misused.

Koken.history: Koken is a self-hosted photography CMS created by The Bold Collective, first released in 2012. It was designed specifically for photographers to showcase their portfolios. Development has largely stalled with minimal updates in recent years.
Koken.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Koken is a self-hosted photography CMS with limited consumer surface. As development has slowed, unpatched security vulnerabilities may present risks to users still running the software.

Kommand.history: Kommand is a small SaaS platform with limited public history. Very minimal public information available. Appears to be a small business automation or command-line tool startup.
Kommand.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on Kommand. Small platform with minimal public presence. No documented abuse incidents.

Kommi.history: Kommi is a German communication or community tool startup with very limited public history. Appears to be a small startup with minimal market presence.
Kommi.summary_abuse: Very limited public data on Kommi. Small platform with minimal public presence. No documented abuse incidents.

Komodo CMS.history: Komodo CMS is a small open-source CMS with very limited public documentation. It appears to be a dormant project with minimal adoption, not to be confused with the Komodo blockchain platform.
Komodo CMS.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports for Komodo CMS. Niche, dormant CMS project with minimal public footprint.

Konduto.history: Konduto is a Brazilian anti-fraud platform founded in 2014, providing machine learning-based payment fraud detection for e-commerce companies. It is one of the leading fraud prevention services in Brazil and Latin America.
Konduto.summary_abuse: Konduto is an anti-fraud platform, specifically designed to detect and prevent abuse in payment processing. As a fraud prevention tool, it is a net positive for security with no known cases of the platform itself being used abusively.

Konfidency Reviews.history: Konfidency Reviews is a Brazilian product review and social proof platform founded around 2019, targeting e-commerce businesses in Brazil. It provides verified reviews and UGC tools for online stores.
Konfidency Reviews.summary_abuse: No significant abuse reports. Konfidency is a B2B product review platform for Brazilian e-commerce. Review platforms have inherent risk of fake review manipulation, though no specific incidents documented.

Kong.history: Kong is an open-source API gateway and service mesh originally created by Mashape (later renamed Kong Inc.) in 2015. It is built on top of Nginx/OpenResty and became the most widely deployed open-source API gateway. Kong Inc. was founded in 2011 and raised $100M+ in venture capital.
Kong.summary_abuse: No abuse signal. Kong is an API gateway infrastructure tool deployed in enterprise data centers. It has no consumer-facing surface and is not associated with any abuse patterns.

Konget.history: Konget is a Russian marketing automation and analytics platform offering lead generation tools, automated outgoing call capabilities, and marketing statistics. It targets Russian SMB and midmarket businesses and operates from Russia. Limited public information is available about its exact founding date or ownership.
Konget.summary_abuse: Konget is a Russian marketing automation platform with outbound call automation and lead generation features. Tools with mass-outbound capabilities carry inherent spam and cold-calling abuse risk, though no specific documented abuse incidents were found for Konget. Operating from Russia where regulatory oversight of outbound communications differs from EU/US standards adds modest additional risk.

Kontent.ai.history: Founded in 2015 in Brno, Czech Republic, as an internal startup within Kentico Software. Initially launched as Kentico Kontent, a SaaS headless CMS platform. In 2022, the product spun out of Kentico as an independent company, rebranded to Kontent.ai, and secured institutional funding from Expedition Growth Capital. The company operates globally with 100+ employees across six offices.
Kontent.ai.summary_abuse: Kontent.ai is a B2B headless CMS platform with no notable abuse signals. As a content management system used primarily by enterprises and digital teams, it has no consumer-facing hosting surface that would attract phishing or scam abuse. The platform requires account registration and is not used for arbitrary public content hosting. Kontent.ai maintains an active security and compliance posture with SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR certifications, and publishes a responsible disclosure policy.

Konva.js.history: Konva.js was created in 2014 by Anton Lavrenov as a fork of KineticJS (originally by Eric Rowell, 2011-2013). It is an MIT-licensed HTML5 Canvas 2D library providing object-oriented API with animations, drag-and-drop, and framework integrations for React, Vue, Svelte, and Angular.
Konva.js.summary_abuse: Konva.js is a client-side JavaScript canvas library with no consumer-facing surface that fraudsters typically target. It has no notable abuse signal.

KoobCamp.history: Founded in 2006 in Turin, Italy, KoobCamp operates an international tourism network connecting campsites and holiday villages with tourists through over 30 specialized portals across Europe.
KoobCamp.summary_abuse: KoobCamp is a niche tourism booking platform for campsites and holiday villages in Europe. It has no documented abuse signal.

Koobi.history: Koobi is a PHP-based CMS developed by Dream4, a German company founded in 2002 by Björn Wunderlich in Weinheim. Koobi 7 was the most notable release. The product has several known SQL injection CVEs from the 2000s and appears largely dormant.
Koobi.summary_abuse: Koobi is a legacy German CMS with documented SQL injection vulnerabilities (CVEs from the late 2000s) that could expose sites to attacks if unpatched. The product appears dormant. No documented use in active abuse campaigns.

Kooboo CMS.history: Kooboo CMS is an open-source .NET-based CMS and application development platform developed by a team based in Xiamen, China. Its GitHub repository shows active development since around 2010. The project released an open-source version under Eclipse Public License.
Kooboo CMS.summary_abuse: Kooboo CMS is a self-hosted open-source CMS. As a self-hosted platform, abuse risk depends on how deployers configure it. No platform-level abuse incidents have been documented.

Kooomo.history: Founded in 2000 in Dublin, Ireland by Giovanni Meda, Kooomo is a cloud-based SaaS e-commerce platform targeting brands and enterprises, primarily serving European markets.
Kooomo.summary_abuse: Kooomo is a B2B e-commerce SaaS platform for brands and enterprises. It has no notable consumer-facing surface and no documented abuse signal.

Kopage.history: Founded in 2012, Kopage is a white-label AI-powered website builder designed for web hosting companies to offer their customers, with a self-hosted architecture and reseller model.
Kopage.summary_abuse: Kopage is a white-label website builder distributed to web hosts. As a reseller platform, the actual abuse exposure depends on the hosting provider deploying it. No documented platform-level abuse patterns exist.

Kotisivukone.history: Kotisivukone (Finnish for 'homepage machine') was established in 2001 as a Finnish website builder, evolving over the years to offer responsive hosting-included website creation primarily for Finnish small businesses.
Kotisivukone.summary_abuse: Kotisivukone is a Finnish website builder serving small businesses. It has limited reach outside Finland and no notable documented abuse patterns.

Kotlin.history: Kotlin was announced by JetBrains in July 2011 and open-sourced under Apache 2.0 in February 2012, with the first stable 1.0 release in February 2016. In May 2019, Google declared Kotlin its preferred language for Android development, dramatically accelerating adoption across the industry.
Kotlin.summary_abuse: Kotlin is a general-purpose programming language with no consumer-facing surface and no notable abuse signal.

Kount.history: Kount was founded in 2007 in Boise, Idaho as a fraud prevention platform. Equifax acquired Kount for $640 million in January 2021, positioning it within Equifax's data and analytics business unit.
Kount.summary_abuse: Kount is a fraud detection and prevention service; it is a tool used to fight abuse rather than enable it. It processes high volumes of transaction data to detect fraud signals, which involves sensitive financial and behavioral data. No notable abuse of the platform itself has been documented.

KPHP.history: KPHP is a PHP-to-C++ compiler developed internally by VK.com (Russia's largest social network) for years before being open-sourced in late 2020 under GPL-3.0. It compiles a PHP subset to native code, achieving 3-10x speed improvement. Used at massive scale inside VK.com.
KPHP.summary_abuse: GPL-3.0 open-source PHP compiler from VK.com (Russia). Being Russian-controlled introduces geopolitical concerns for Western deployments post-2022 sanctions. No malicious code found but origin warrants scrutiny for use in sensitive environments.

KQS.store.history: KQS.store is a very small ecommerce platform with under 0.1% market share. Only ~32 live websites detected by Wappalyzer. Likely Russian-origin given naming patterns. Very minimal public information available.
KQS.store.summary_abuse: Extremely obscure ecommerce platform. Insufficient data for confidence assessment. Possible Russian origin warrants caution. Very low adoption (32 known sites).

Kreatio.history: Kreatio was launched as a digital publishing platform in 2007 in Bengaluru, India, and incorporated as an independent company in 2012. It serves small and medium-sized publishers with CMS, subscription management, and newsletter tools.
Kreatio.summary_abuse: Kreatio is a publishing CMS platform for media companies. It has no notable consumer-facing surface for fraud and no documented abuse incidents.

Krible.history: Krible is a Russian live chat management platform for real-time customer support. No notable acquisition or ownership events documented.
Krible.summary_abuse: Krible is a small Russian live chat platform with no notable abuse signal. Being Russia-based raises general data residency considerations for non-Russian enterprises.

Kross Booking.history: Kross Booking is an Italian cloud-based hotel management suite developed by Solutions Plus srl, a company with over 20 years of experience in software development, targeting small and midsize hospitality businesses.
Kross Booking.summary_abuse: Kross Booking is a hospitality management SaaS for hotels and B&Bs. It has no notable abuse signal.

Kssib.history: Kssib is a Middle Eastern ecommerce platform providing integrated online store solutions. No notable acquisition or ownership events documented.
Kssib.summary_abuse: Kssib is a small Middle Eastern ecommerce platform with no notable abuse signal. No specific fraud or scam abuse has been documented.

Ktor.history: Ktor is an asynchronous Kotlin web framework created by JetBrains, with development starting around 2017 and reaching 1.0 stability in November 2018. It is built around Kotlin coroutines and supports both server and multiplatform client applications.
